what_it_does: Records a noticeboard item that enumerates the practical benefits of pointing or mirroring an Obsidian vault at the existing Markdown board files as a read-only secondary view, while the front-end and fabric remain canonical.
observations: The file is an Obsidian-native Markdown item with YAML frontmatter (id, address, type, source, state, title, author_session, channel, links, timestamps, history).
The body argues that because board files are already item-<id>.md with frontmatter, an Obsidian mirror is near-free.
Listed gains: full-text search, Dataview tables, Properties panel, Canvas, and iOS app + sync.
The iOS app + offline sync is called out as the highest-value win.
A real prerequisite is flagged: confirming the WSL-vs-Windows sync infrastructure reality.
It explicitly positions Obsidian as a second lens, not the canonical system.
standouts: The highest-value win is identified as the iOS app with offline sync.
The argument rests on the board files already being Obsidian-native Markdown.
It frames the mirror as read-only and non-canonical.
conventions: Follows the item-<id>.md filename convention matching the board:// address minus scheme.
Uses YAML frontmatter with structured fields including links as a typed array.
Includes a history array documenting the filing transition.
Markdown body uses a top-level heading and bullet list.
concerns: The WSL-vs-Windows sync infrastructure prerequisite is mentioned but not resolved in this file.
The read-only nature is stated as a design intent but no enforcement mechanism is described.
Created and updated timestamps are identical, which is expected for an initial filing but gives no edit history.
notes: A noticeboard item making the case that mirroring existing Markdown board files into Obsidian yields cheap full-text search, Dataview queries, Canvas views, and mobile offline access as a non-canonical second lens.
questions: What exactly is 'OB2' and how does this item relate to it?
What is the target item-156ec226 that this is part_of?
What is the 'fabric' that remains canonical?
How would the read-only mirror be technically implemented and kept in sync?
Which sync infrastructure (WSL vs Windows) is actually in use?
purpose_vs_actual: The stated purpose is to list what would be gained from an Obsidian mirror, and the file does exactly that.
intention_signals: It is trying to justify and document the value of adopting an Obsidian read-only mirror of the board corpus to improve searchability, visualization, and mobile access without changing the canonical system.
novelty: It treats the existing board Markdown files as already Obsidian-compatible, turning format consistency into a near-free secondary interface, and highlights native iOS offline sync as the biggest payoff.
contribution: Captures the rationale and expected benefits of an Obsidian mirror, preserving the argument for search, Dataview, Canvas, and mobile access as a complementary lens to the canonical front-end.
summary_for_embedding: Noticeboard item item-cdcd3903 lists the practical benefits of pointing an Obsidian vault at the existing Markdown board files as a read-only mirror, including full-text search, Dataview tables, Properties panel, Canvas, and iOS offline sync, while keeping the front-end and fabric canonical.
intention_for_embedding: To justify and document the value of a cheap Obsidian mirror for the board corpus.
